---
title: "Lesson 5: Expectation and Properties of Expectation"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_expectation-and-properties-of-expectation.mp4 >}}

- [transcript](01_expectation-and-properties-of-expectation.en.txt){target="_blank"}
- [Handout](02_video-slides-for-expectation-and-properties-of-expectation_module1_lesson4.pdf){width=700 height=700}
:::


::: {#exm-expectation-discrete}
### Expectation of a Tabular RV
Consider the discrete random variable $X$:

| $x$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $P(X=x)$ | $\frac14$ | $\frac14$ | $\frac12$ |

Its expectation is

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] 
&= (1)\left(\frac14\right) + (2)\left(\frac14\right) + (3)\left(\frac12\right) \\
&= \frac94 \\
&= 2.25
\end{aligned}
$$ 
:::

::: {#def-expectation-discrete}
### Expectation: discrete case

For a discrete random variable $X$,

$$\mathbb{E}[X]
= \sum_x x\,P(X=x).
$$
:::

::: {#def-expectation-continuous}
### Expectation: continuous case

For a continuous random variable $X$ with density $f_X(x)$,

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f_X(x) dx \qquad
$$ {#eq-expectation-continuous}
:::

---

::: {#def-expectation-mean}
## Notation for Expectation

The expectation of $X$ is often denoted by

$$
\mu = \mathbb{E}[X]
$$

or, when several random variables are present,

$$
\mu_X = \mathbb{E}[X],
\qquad
\mu_Y = \mathbb{E}[Y].
$$
:::


::: {#exm-normal-expectation}
### Expectation for a Normal RV

Let $X \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X]
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\,f_X(x)\,dx \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
x\,
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\!\left\{
-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right\}
\,dx \\
&= \cdots \\
&= \mu
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

::: {#exm-exponential-expectation}
### Exponential Distribution

Let $X \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda), \qquad \lambda > 0$ where $\lambda$ is the rate parameter. Then

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] 
    &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\,f_X(x)\,dx \\
    &= \int_{-\infty}^{0} x\cdot 0\,dx + \int_{0}^{\infty} x\,\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx \\
    &= \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

::: {#exm-exponential-expectation-2}
### Train interarrival times

If $X$ is the train interarrival time and the arrival rate is $2$ per hour, then $\lambda=2$, so the average time between arrivals is

$$
\mathbb{E}[X]  = \frac12 \text{ hour}.
$$
:::

### Rate Versus Mean Parameterization of the Exponential Distribution {#sec-exponential-parameterization}

The notation $X \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)$ is ambiguous unless the parameterization is specified.

::: {#def-exponential-rate}
### Exponential distribution: rate parameterization

If $X \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)$ means that $\lambda$ is a rate parameter, then

$$
f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x),
$$

and

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

Equivalently, one may write $X \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\operatorname{rate}=\lambda).$
:::

::: {#def-exponential-mean}
### Exponential distribution: mean parameterization

If $X \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)$ means that $\lambda$ is a mean parameter, then

$$
f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-x/\lambda}\mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x),
$$

and

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \lambda.
$$

Equivalently, one may write

$$
X \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\operatorname{mean}=\lambda).
$$
:::

### Expectation of a Function of a Random Variable {#sec-expectation-function}

Suppose

- $X$ has probability density function $f_X(x)$;
- $g$ is a function.

We want to find $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]$ 

Let $Y = g(X)$ if $g$ is one-to-one and differentiable, then the density of $Y$ is

$$
f_Y(y) = f_X \left(g^{-1}(y)\right) \left| \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y) \right|.
$$ 

Therefore,

$$
\mathbb{E}[g(X)] =  \mathbb{E}[Y] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\,f_Y(y)\,dy.
$$

### Law of the Unconscious Statistician {#sec-lotus}

Continuing with $Y=g(X)$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[g(X)]
&=
\mathbb{E}[Y] \\
&=
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\,f_Y(y)\,dy \\
&=
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
y\,
f_X\!\left(g^{-1}(y)\right)
\left|
\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)
\right|
\,dy
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $x = g^{-1}(y)$ then

$$
dx = \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)\,dy.
$$

Substituting back gives

$$
\mathbb{E}[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x)f_X(x)\,dx.
$$

::: {#thm-lotus}
### Law of the Unconscious Statistician

For a continuous random variable $X$ with density $f_X(x)$,

$$
\mathbb{E}[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x)f_X(x)\,dx.
$$

Thus, to compute $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]$, one does not need to first derive the distribution of $Y=g(X)$.
:::

### Law of the Unconscious Statistician: Summary

The same result is emphasized again:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[g(X)]
&= \mathbb{E}[Y] \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\,f_Y(y)\,dy \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_X\!\left(g^{-1}(y)\right) \left|
\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y) \right| \,dy.
\end{aligned}
$$

With

$$
x = g^{-1}(y), \qquad dx = \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)\,dy,
$$

we get

$$
\mathbb{E}[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x)f_X(x)\,dx.
$$


::: {#exm-expectation-discrete-function}
### Discrete Example Revisited

Again let $X$ be given by

| $x$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $P(X=x)$ | $\frac14$ | $\frac14$ | $\frac12$ |

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X]
&= (1)\left(\frac14\right) + (2)\left(\frac14\right) + (3)\left(\frac12\right) \\
&= \frac94 = 2.25.
\end{aligned}
$$ 
:::

### Continuous Example

For a continuous random variable,

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\,f_X(x)\,dx.
$$ {#eq-expectation-continuous}

If

$$
X \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)
$$

under the rate parameterization, then

$$
f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x).
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X]
&= \int_0^\infty x\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Expectation of a Function of Two Random Variables

Let $(X,Y)$ have joint density $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$. Then

$$
\mathbb{E}[g(X,Y)]
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x,y)f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy
$$

### Expectation is a Linear Operator

::: {#thm-linearity-expectation }
### Linearity of expectation

For random variables $X$ and $Y$,

$$
\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]
$$ {#eq-linearity-expectation}
:::

::: {.proof}

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X+Y]
&=
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x+y)f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy \\
&=
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy
+ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy
\end{aligned}
$$

For the first term,

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy = f_X(x),
$$

so

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
x f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy\,dx
&=
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
x f_X(x)\,dx \\
&=
\mathbb{E}[X].
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, the second term is $\mathbb{E}[Y]$. Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y] \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

### Expectation and Independence

::: {#thm-product-expectation-independent .theorem}
## Product expectation under independence

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then

$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y].
$$
:::

::: {.proof}

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[XY]
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xy\,f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xy\,f_X(x)f_Y(y)\,dx\,dy
\qquad \text{(by independence)} \\
&= \left( \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f_X(x)\,dx \right) \left( \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y f_Y(y)\,dy \right) \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y] \\
\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

### Further Properties

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then for suitable functions $g$ and $h$,

$$
\mathbb{E}[g(X)h(Y)] = \mathbb{E}[g(X)]\mathbb{E}[h(Y)].
$$

However,

$$
\mathbb{E}[XY]
=
\mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]
$$

does **not** imply that $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

### Example: Product Expectation Without Independence

Let $X$ have distribution

| $x$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $P(X=x)$ | $\frac14$ | $\frac12$ | $\frac14$ |

Then

$$
\mathbb{E}[X]
= (-1)\left(\frac14\right) + 0\left(\frac12\right) + 1\left(\frac14\right)
= 0
$$

Therefore,

$$
\mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y] = 0
$$

for any random variable $Y$ with finite expectation.

Define $Y = X^2$. Then $Y$ is dependent on $X$, since $Y$ is determined by $X$. Yet

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[XY]
&= \mathbb{E}[X^3] \\
&= (-1)^3\left(\frac14\right) + 0^3\left(\frac12\right) + 1^3\left(\frac14\right) \\
&= -\frac14 + 0 + \frac14 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus

$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]
$$ 

even though $X$ and $Y$ are not independent.

### Reflection

1. Expectation are just a weighted average of the possible values of a random variable.
2. Expectation is a linear operator, 
3. The expectation of a product of independent random variables is the product of their expectations. (However, the converse is not true: if the expectation of a product equals the product of expectations, it is not sufficient to show independence.)
4. Independence and the ability to factor the joint density into a product of marginal densities is a powerful property that arises from the structure of the measurable space and the definition of independence. 
5. The notation $X \sim Y$ is ambiguous in most cases unless the **parameterization** is specified:
    - We saw that the $\text{Exponential}$ distribution can be parameterized by either the rate or the mean, and that the expectation depends on which parameterization is used.
    - The $\mathcal{\Gamma}{\alpha, \beta}$ distribution is another example of a distribution that can be parameterized in multiple ways, and the expectation depends on the parameterization.
    - It is very likely that different parameterizations are due to different generative stories and these allow a deeper understanding of the underlying process that generates the data.
    - In my experience parameterization is often a real source of confusion for practitioners as well as student. I've wasted time trying get the poissone and exponential distributions to match up due to bad parameterization.
6. In the section on the law of the unconscious statistician, we saw that without the theorem, the correct way to compute the expectation of a function of a random variable is to first derive the distribution of the new random variable and then compute its expectation. However, we showed that the  [law of the unconscious statistician](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_of_the_unconscious_statistician) allows us to use the same form of expectation as we used  i.e. @eq-expectation-continuous bur replacing X with g(X). This seems like a tautology, but it is actually a very powerful result that allows us to compute the expectation of a function of a random variable without having to derive the distribution of that function. This is especially useful when the function is complicated or when the distribution of the function is not known.
